states:
This is Visual Studio Code, with its 1.117 release notes filling the main editor pane while two terminal panes run concurrently at the bottom — one launching a Uvicorn backend server on port 8000 via `uvicorn main:app --reload`, the other starting an Electron app called `storysymbiosis@0.2.0` which has just been granted screen recording permission. The file tree on the left reveals a project structured around a `backend` Python service and frontend files (`.html`, `.js`, `.md`), inside a folder called `storysymbiosis` within a broader `whiteboarding-ai` projects directory.

Overlaid on the right is a distinctive floating panel titled "your audience" containing character avatars — Remi (Recombinability), Case (Continuity?), Dex (Discoverability), Rex (Review?) — each rendered as a soft pastel creature with a personality label, alongside a "VOICE" slider

The environment is Visual Studio Code displaying the 1.117 release notes in the main editor, with two terminals running simultaneously at the bottom — a Uvicorn backend on port 8000 and an Electron app `storysymbiosis@0.2.0` that has just received screen recording permission. The file tree shows a `storysymbiosis` project containing a Python `backend`, frontend files (`.html`, `.js`, `.md`), sitting within a broader `whiteboarding-ai` parent directory. Floating over the right side of the screen is the app itself — a panel titled "your audience" populated with pastel creature avatars, each tied to a reviewing persona: Remi for Recombinability, Case for Continuity, Dex for Discoverability, Rex for Review, and a "VOICE" slider running from quiet through balanced to open.

The person is building a creative companion tool that watches their screen work and reflects it back through a c

The environment is a web browser displaying a timed-out MyBU Student login page — a mostly empty gray screen with a "Sign in to MyBU Student" button in the center, topped by a bookmarks bar crowded with research-oriented tabs (LegiSim, mech-interp, BCH, CDSLab, Courses, STRIPED, "efficient multi-head", "MVE neural net", a Grad folder with a melting-face emoji). Floating over the right edge is the same "your audience" panel from before, now the only active creative surface visible: Remi (Recombinability), Case (Continuity), Dex (Discoverability), Rex (Review), each rendered as a soft pastel creature, with the VOICE slider sitting near "balanced."

The bookmarks betray the person's broader life — a graduate student or researcher juggling coursework logins, mechanistic interpretability reading, lab directories, and paper drafts —

The environment is Google Slides, displaying a single slide titled "StorySymbiosis: Interactive AI audiences and provocateurs to support crea[tive]..." — a project pitch or research poster slide rendered in a hand-drawn, sketchbook aesthetic on warm cream paper. The central illustration is a pencil-sketched tablet or browser mockup with two pink-purple panels on the right: an upper panel showing four small avatar-circles with numbered badges (a stand-in for the "your audience" creature panel from before), and a lower panel depicting a cultural artifact card with an image and caption. Annotation lines point outward to handwritten-style labels: "Traditional storyboarding, brainstorming, narrative prototyping environment" on the left canvas area, a partially obscured note about virtual audiences, and "System surfaces cultural artifacts and affordances for creative inspiration in real-time" on the right.

The environment is Google Slides, now displaying a single landscape slide titled "StorySymbiosis: Interactive AI audiences and provocateurs to support crea[tive]..." — a research project pitch or concept slide rendered in a deliberately hand-drawn, sketchy aesthetic on a warm cream background. The centerpiece is a mocked-up browser window drawn in pencil-like line art, with a large hatched workspace on the left annotated "Traditional storyboarding, brainstorming, narrative prototyping environment," and two pink/purple panels on the right: one showing clustered avatar bubbles with numbered indicators (the AI audience members reacting), and another showing what appears to be a cultural artifact card annotated "System surfaces cultural artifacts and affordances for creative inspiration in real-time." The floating "your audience" panel from before is still anchored at the right edge, now clearly revealed as a live U

The environment is Google Slides, now showing what appears to be a title slide for the same research project, but with a notably renamed concept: "AudienceSymbiosis" (previously "StorySymbiosis"), rendered in the same deliberately hand-drawn, marker-like display typeface underlined for emphasis. Below the title sits a boxed subtitle articulating the thesis with more academic precision than before — "A sociocultural affordance, human-centered, adapter system to enhance user creativity and prototyping" — and beneath that, two juxtaposed visual anchors: a photograph of a real human audience on the left and a row of four sketch-style robot/character figures on the right, visually proposing the core metaphor of synthetic audiences standing in for or augmenting human ones. The floating "your audience" UI panel still hovers at the right edge, now showing distinct agent personas (Remi tagged RECOMBINABILITY, D

The environment is Google Slides, displaying the title slide of an academic or research presentation on a cream-colored background. The title "AudienceSymbiosis" dominates in a hand-drawn, underlined marker typeface, paired with a framed subtitle defining the concept as "A sociocultural affordance, human-centered, adapter system to enhance user creativity and prototyping." Below, a photograph of a dense human crowd sits opposite four sketched robot-like figures, visually staging the central metaphor: synthetic agents as stand-ins or companions for real audiences. A floating "Rex / Reviewability" panel now occupies the right edge, offering meta-commentary — it has "NOTICED" the rename from "StorySymbiosis" to "AudienceSymbiosis" and reads this as reflecting a deeper conceptual maturation toward synthetic-human audience relationships, while prompting the user for a reaction via "

The environment is Google Slides, showing the same title slide of a research presentation titled "AudienceSymbiosis," positioned as "A sociocultural affordance, human-centered, adapter system to enhance user creativity and prototyping." The cream slide retains its established visual rhetoric: a hand-drawn marker-style title, a photograph of a real crowd on the left, and four sketched robot-like figures on the right, staging the human-versus-synthetic audience dichotomy at the heart of the project.

The meaningful change from the prior state is in the floating right-edge panel, which has shifted from a single "Rex / Reviewability" commentator to what now reads as a cast of personified audience agents under the header "your audience." Visible are Remi (Recombinability) with a purple rabbit-like avatar and Dex (Discoverability) with a blue avatar, and a "VOICE" slider beneath them running from "quiet" to "

The environment is Google Slides, with multiple overlapping panels from what appears to be a companion feedback tool (the "Dex / Discoverability" agent interface) layered on top of the editing canvas. The visible slide has shifted meaningfully from the prior title slide to an explanatory content slide titled "How does StoryScaffold work?"—indicating the deck's project name may actually be StoryScaffold, with AudienceSymbiosis functioning as the broader framework or a parallel concept. The slide lays out a three-step mechanic in numbered prose: audience agents intermittently spectate the user's work environment, environment changes or interaction data trigger those audiences, and embodied members then offer tailored questions or resources to inspire the user. On the right, a hand-drawn diagram makes this pipeline concrete: stacked "session snapshots" feed into a dense blob labeled "vision language model processes session snapshots," which then perform

The environment is Google Slides, with a live companion panel from the StoryScaffold tool itself visible on the right edge—showing audience agents "Remi (Recombinability)" and "Dex (Discoverability)" alongside a voice slider calibrated between "quiet" and "balanced." The slide titled "How does StoryScaffold work?" articulates the system's three-step mechanic in parallel numbered prose, paired with a hand-drawn pipeline diagram on the right: session snapshots stack into a vision-language model (rendered as a cluster of blue circles), which then branches out via arrows to four embodied robot-like characters standing on geometric pedestals. The creative decisions visible here commit firmly to a particular aesthetic register—handwritten-style typography, loose sketched illustration, and a warm cream background—which softens what is essentially a technical systems diagram into something more approachable and storybook-like. The numbered text

The environment is still Google Slides, but a Raycast-style application launcher has been invoked over the canvas, partially obscuring the slide with a dark translucent command palette showing suggestions for WhatsApp, Code, Messages, Slack, and a file search command—the user has typed just the letter "c." Behind this overlay, the slide's title has changed to "A visual demo," and the three numbered points now describe a more concrete mechanic: audience agents spectating the work environment, environment/user triggers, and something beginning with "Embo—" (likely "Embody" or "Embodied") involving tailoring resources. The hand-drawn "session snapshots" illustration of stacked pages persists in the upper right, but the rest of the pipeline diagram appears to have been removed or is not visible in this crop, suggesting the slide has been simplified to set up a live demonstration rather than explain the full system abstractly. The creative direction has shifted

audit.comment_full: NOTICED: The project title has shifted from "StorySymbiosis" to "AudienceSymbiosis," reflecting a deeper conceptual evolution. The work has matured to emphasize the relationship between synthetic audiences and their human counterparts, suggesting a more nuanced exploration of how these interactions enhance creativity and prototyping.

audit.comment_full: This recalls the work of the late 20th-century interactive artist Jenny Holzer, particularly her "Truisms" series, which used language and technology to engage audiences in dialogue — a fitting parallel that unlocks questions about how these synthetic agents can provoke real-time responses and reflections, enhancing creative processes in ways akin to interactive public art.

audit.comment_full: The intertwining of technical information with a storybook-like aesthetic creates a bridge between complex systems and user engagement, akin to a narrative weaving through a dense academic text. This presentation style invites the audience to not only understand the mechanics but also imagine the potential emotional journeys within the creative process — much like a character arc in a story. What other narrative techniques could be employed to deepen this engagement?
